The **Royal Grammar School Worcester** (also known as **RGS Worcester** and **RGSW**) is an 11-18 [co-educational](/wiki/Mixed-sex_education "Mixed-sex education"), [private](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom") [day school](/wiki/Day_school "Day school") and [sixth form](/wiki/Sixth_form "Sixth form") in [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England"), [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire "Worcestershire"), England. Founded before 1291, and possibly as early as 685, it is one of the oldest British independent day schools.

In September 2007, the school merged with the neighbouring [Alice Ottley School](/wiki/The_Alice_Ottley_School "The Alice Ottley School") and was briefly known as **RGS Worcester and The Alice Ottley School (RGSAO)** before reverting to the original name. The school began accepting girls in 2003, prior to the merger. The school currently consists of the main secondary school and three preparatory campuses known as [RGS Springfield](/wiki/RGS_Springfield "RGS Springfield") (previously a boarding house of the Alice Ottley School),[[3]](#cite_note-3) [RGS The Grange](/wiki/RGS_The_Grange "RGS The Grange") (opened 1996),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and RGS Dodderhill.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Until 1992 it accepted [boarders](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school"). Boarding pupils would reside in Whiteladies House, a building that is rumoured to contain hidden treasure from [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") from when he sought refuge there during the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6) It is now a day school and in 2024 was named the top co-ed independent school in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(region) "West Midlands (region)").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Tracing its origins back to the 7th century, it is the sixth [oldest school in the world](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_schools_in_the_world "List of the oldest schools in the world").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Grammar school § History](/wiki/Grammar_school#History "Grammar school"); [Latin school](/wiki/Latin_school "Latin school"); and [Neo-Latin § Latin in school education, 1500–1700](/wiki/Neo-Latin#Latin_in_school_education,_1500–1700 "Neo-Latin")

The school was founded as a secular [monastic](/wiki/Monastic "Monastic") school in [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England") in around 685 by [Bishop Bosel](/wiki/Bosel "Bosel").[[8]](#cite_note-8) This makes it the 6th [oldest school](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_schools_in_the_world "List of the oldest schools in the world") in the world. It was located outside the monastic precincts (as with [the King's School, Canterbury](/wiki/The_King%27s_School,_Canterbury "The King's School, Canterbury")) and catered for the relatives of monks and children intending to go into the monastery. The first written reference to the school appears in 1265 when the [Bishop of Worcester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Worcester "Bishop of Worcester"), [Walter de Cantilupe](/wiki/Walter_de_Cantilupe "Walter de Cantilupe"), sent four chaplains into the city to teach.[[9]](#cite_note-Fol-9)

Conclusive evidence appears in 1291 when an argument was settled by Bishop [Godfrey Giffard](/wiki/Godfrey_Giffard "Godfrey Giffard") regarding who owned the wax from the candles used at the feast of St Swithun. It was decided that the scholars of the Worcester School owned it, and the Rector of Saint Nicholas Church had to rely on the generosity of the scholars in order to get candle wax. The headmaster is mentioned as Stephen of London. The letter dated December 1291 is in the County Records Office in Worcester.

The next headmaster was appointed in 1312 as Hugh of Northampton as recorded in the Bishop's register for that year. He was appointed personally by the Bishop of Worcester, [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") [Walter Reynolds](/wiki/Walter_Reynolds "Walter Reynolds"). The school continued to exist under the control of the city guilds through the centuries with various records of headmasters being appointed, again listed in the registers of the bishops of Worcester. One in particular was 'Sir Richard (Chaplain)', who was dismissed by the bishop of Worcester, Philip Morgan, in 1422 for taking money from the scholars for his own use. He was replaced the same year by Sir John Bredel. Sir Richard Pennington was appointed in 1485 and is known to have given money to the Archbishop of Canterbury's fund,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] showing the strong connection of the school with the church.

### Royal charters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Royal charters")]

After a petition by some notable citizens of Worcester to endow the school permanently, the school was given a [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter") by [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") in 1561 and a governing body known as the Six Masters was set up, which remains as the governing body of the new [RGS Worcester](/wiki/RGS_Worcester "RGS Worcester") school today. Amongst famous Six masters are [John Wall](/wiki/John_Wall_(priest_and_martyr) "John Wall (priest and martyr)"), [Earl Beauchamp](/wiki/Earl_Beauchamp "Earl Beauchamp"), [Charles William Dyson Perrins](/wiki/Charles_William_Dyson_Perrins "Charles William Dyson Perrins") and Anthony Lechemere.[[9]](#cite_note-Fol-9)

The Six Masters acquired much land for the school including its current site bought in 1562, the Pitchcroft fields, now used as the city racecourse, and land in Herefordshire still owned by the school. The 1906 Charity Commission survey also recorded a number of pubs in Worcester which still exist today.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)

A second Royal Charter was granted in 1843 by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom"), and the title of 'Royal' was conferred in 1869.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Houses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Houses")]

The [house system](/wiki/House_system "House system") was introduced in 1899 by the then headmaster Frederick Arthur Hillard. Initially six houses were established, and membership of houses was based on the place of residence of each boy. The original houses were: Boarders, Barbourne, City, St. John's, County A, and County B. In 1909 the house system was changed to reflect the increasing number of boys in the school, and the difficulty of allocating pupils on the basis of where they lived. The six houses created in 1909 were: School House, for boarders, (which, due to common usage, changed to Whiteladies, as this was the building in which the boarders lived); Temple (after Henry Temple, headmaster 1850s); Tudor (after [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England")); Woolfe (after Richard Woolfe, benefactor 1877 ); Wylde (after [Thomas Wylde](/wiki/Thomas_Wylde_(clothier) "Thomas Wylde (clothier)"), benefactor 1558); and Yewle (after Robert Yewle, Six Master 1561). In 1963 two additional houses were created by the then headmaster Godfrey Brown, namely Langley (after William Langley, Six Master 1561) and Moore (after John Moore, benefactor 1626).[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)

In 2013/ 2014, the house system changed from six houses (Flagge-red for Flagge Meadow, Butler-yellow for the old Butler Library, Cobham-gold for Cobham Hall, Eld-green for Eld Hall, Perrins-orange for Perrins Hall and Britannia-blue for Britannia house) to the current house system.

The current houses are:

| Whiteladies |  |

The school holds a yearly house championship, decided by a range of events throughout the school year in which all four houses compete, with the winners of each event being awarded four points, the second-placed house three, down to the losing house one point. The house championship was traditionally called the 'Cock House' (or Cock House Cup Competition[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)) competition, its name deriving from that of the [Cock](/wiki/Rooster "Rooster"). The original cup that was competed for is one which was presented to the school in 1902 by the Old Elizabethans' Association; in modern times competition is for a cup which was introduced in 1978.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Affiliated schools")]

### RGS The Grange

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: RGS The Grange")]

**[RGS The Grange](/wiki/RGS_The_Grange "RGS The Grange") (Royal Grammar School The Grange)** is located in [Claines](/wiki/Claines "Claines"), north of Worcester, United Kingdom. It is approximately three miles (4.8 km) north of the senior school, and is situated on a 50 acres (20 ha) site.

The headmaster of the school since 2009 has been Gareth Hughes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The school was founded in 1996, when RGS Worcester purchased a traditional English farmhouse in Claines named “The Grange”. It opened to just twenty pupils at the time, but soon other parts of the RGS prep school moved from the senior school site to the Grange. By early 2004, all of the prep school was located at the Grange.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Facilities at the school were upgraded in the early 2000s with a £4.5 million development of the buildings. The build was completed ahead of schedule.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Grange has wide-ranging sporting facilities, set in 50 acres (200,000 m2) of maintained pitches and playing fields. It also has an astroturf pitch for hockey.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The school has four houses: Cash (green), Perowne (yellow), Cornwall (red), and Goodrich (blue).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During 2008 the school formed linkages with the community departments of [Worcester Warriors](/wiki/Worcester_Warriors "Worcester Warriors") Rugby Club and [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa "Aston Villa") Football Club.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

**[RGS Springfield](/wiki/RGS_Springfield "RGS Springfield")** (**Royal Grammar School Springfield**) is located in Britannia Square in [Worcester](/wiki/Worcester,_England "Worcester, England"), United Kingdom. It is located 100 yards west of the Senior School.

After the RGS/AO merger, RGS Springfield became [mixed-sex](/wiki/Mixed-sex_education "Mixed-sex education").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The headmistress of the school is Mrs Laura Brown.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Prior to September 2009, the school was known as AO Springfield, as it was the AO's junior school prior to the RGS/AO merger. Today, the school houses 150 pupils.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

RGS Springfield is located in a large [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") house surrounded by six acres of gardens and playing fields. The school has a forest area where the children can learn about nature.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Academic and music scholarships are offered at RGS Springfield.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Academic scholarships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Academic scholarships")]

These are offered to people entering year five and year six. There is no separate exam; pupils who perform very well in the entrance exam are offered scholarships.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

##### Music scholarships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Music scholarships")]

These are also offered to people entering year three and year five. As a guide, pupils taking a music scholarship exam in Year three are expected to hold Grade one on one musical instrument. Year five pupils taking the exam are expected to be playing two or more instruments and have achieved grade three in at least one of their grades.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

**[RGS Dodderhill](/wiki/RGS_Dodderhill "RGS Dodderhill")** **(Royal Grammar School Dodderhill)** is located in the town of Droitwich Spa, Worcestershire, approximately six miles outside of Worcester.

Previously Dodderhill Independent Girls School, the school merged with RGS Worcester family of schools in 2019 and became known as RGS Dodderhill.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In September 2021, RGS Dodderhill Prep School became co-educational while the Senior School remains for girls only.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The headmistress of the school is Mrs Sarah Atkinson who was appointed in 2019 following the retirement of Mrs Cate Mawston.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

RGS Dodderhill has a large indoor sports hall, outdoor netball and tennis courts, as well as purpose-built drama and music studios.

## Connections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Connections")]

The school is a member of the 'Monmouth Group', a collection of schools similar in aims and membership to that of the [Eton Group](/wiki/Eton_Group "Eton Group"). The school is also a member of the [HMC](/wiki/Headmasters%27_and_Headmistresses%27_Conference "Headmasters' and Headmistresses' Conference").

## Land and buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Land and buildings")]

Many of the current buildings were paid for [Charles William Dyson Perrins](/wiki/Charles_Dyson_Perrins "Charles Dyson Perrins"), who was an [Old Boy](/wiki/Old_boy_network "Old boy network") and a member of the school's governing body. Perrins Hall was named after his father James Dyson Perrins, owner of [Lea and Perrins](/wiki/Lea_and_Perrins "Lea and Perrins") Worcestershire Sauce, who went to the school.

The basement of Perrins Hall contains a rifle range, which was added in 1914.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6) The back rooms of Perrins Hall used to be the sixth-form common rooms and are now used for storage.

### Flagge Meadow

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Flagge Meadow")]

Flagge Meadow is located nearby at the back of the school, next to the [Worcester and Birmingham Canal](/wiki/Worcester_and_Birmingham_Canal "Worcester and Birmingham Canal").
Flagge Meadow (pronounced Flag) was first levelled and used for [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") in 1886.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6) The first recorded match to be held there was in 1939, when the school played [Merton College, Oxford](/wiki/Merton_College,_Oxford "Merton College, Oxford").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The ground has also played host to several Second XI fixtures for the [Worcestershire Second XI](/wiki/Worcestershire_County_Cricket_Club "Worcestershire County Cricket Club") in the [Second XI Championship](/wiki/Second_XI_Championship "Second XI Championship") and Second XI Trophy.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2007, the ground held a single [List-A](/wiki/List-A_cricket "List-A cricket") match for [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire_County_Cricket_Club "Worcestershire County Cricket Club") when they played [Sri Lanka A](/wiki/Sri_Lanka_A_cricket_team "Sri Lanka A cricket team").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Each year in the summer term cricket is played at Flagge Meadow.

### Other land

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Other land")]

St. Oswald's, the school's second playing field, is located further down the canal and is mainly used for [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)"), [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and [rounders](/wiki/Rounders "Rounders").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## School's halls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: School's halls")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Clock_block_rgs.jpg/250px-Clock_block_rgs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clock_block_rgs.jpg)

Front of the Clock Block.

The Old School buildings were built in 1868 on a site owned by the school since 1562.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6) The Main Hall, Eld Hall and adjoining buildings were designed by A E Perkins in the Gothic style. It is three bays long with a central lantern. A life-size statue of Elizabeth I by R L Boulton stands above the central window.[[15]](#cite_note-Pevs-15)

The Perrins Hall was built in 1914 to the plans of Alfred Hill Parker (an Old Boy) in a Jacobethan style with an [Oriel Window](/wiki/Oriel_window "Oriel window") on the staircase end and balcony looking over the hall. The interior is panelled with fitted bookcases (which make up the [Dowty](/wiki/George_Dowty "George Dowty") Library[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)) and a plastered ceiling. Two war memorials for the two World Wars are housed in the hall and a life-size portrait of Charles William [Dyson Perrins](/wiki/Dyson_Perrins "Dyson Perrins") hangs opposite the fireplace. Portraits of the 20th-century headmasters hang below. The school [organ](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)") is in this building, and is played regularly at assemblies.

The Clock Block is connected to the Perrins Hall and was built in 1927, and had extension work carried out in 1967 to link it to the Science Block. It has a bell tower and clock above the entrance. The clock (which was installed by [J. B. Joyce & Co](/wiki/J._B._Joyce_%26_Co "J. B. Joyce & Co"))[[16]](#cite_note-Pickford1995-16) is made of [Cotswold Limestone](/wiki/Cotswold_stone "Cotswold stone"), and is surmounted by the carved head of [Old Father Time](/wiki/Father_Time "Father Time").[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6) To commemorate the millennium a [stained glass](/wiki/Stained_glass "Stained glass") window was commissioned and installed over the main entrance to the Clock Block.

In the Alice Ottley Building, formerly the main school building for the Alice Ottley School, there are two more halls: Cobham Hall and Main Hall. Main Hall is the school's dining hall, with views over a lawn and a stained glass window commemorating Miss Margaret Spurling, headmistress of the Alice Ottley School from 1912 to 1934.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Long_walk%2C_Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester.jpg/250px-Long_walk%2C_Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Long_walk,_Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester.jpg)

Long walk with the science block in the distance. The small school crest in the foreground features the three Black Pears.

The science buildings were built in 1922 and opened by the [Duke](/wiki/George_VI_of_the_United_Kingdom "George VI of the United Kingdom") and [Duchess of York](/wiki/Elizabeth_Bowes-Lyon "Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon") (later King George VI and Queen Elizabeth).[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6) The science buildings were subsequently refurbished in 1996 and thereafter re-opened by [Michael Portillo](/wiki/Michael_Portillo "Michael Portillo"). The science block features at one end of a long path which comes from the main quad of the school, which is the location of Perrins Hall and the Main block. This long path is known as Long Walk.

Whiteladies House, built in the 17th century, was traditionally the Headmaster's house and stands opposite Clock Block across the gardens. This is currently where the school admin staff, nurse, and school heads of departments are situated. The West wall was part of the Whiteladies Priory Chapel built in 1255.[[17]](#cite_note-Leach-17) Its name derives from the [white habit](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit") that is worn by [Cistercian](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians") nuns, who were based at a Nunnery, which was adjacent to Whiteladies.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Rgs_library.jpg/250px-Rgs_library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rgs_library.jpg)

The school's library, with the old roof structure visible

Other buildings include Priory House (17th Century), Pullinger House (1980s), Gordon House (after [Adam Lindsay Gordon](/wiki/Adam_Lindsay_Gordon "Adam Lindsay Gordon") OE), Stephen Hall (1961, opened by [Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_the_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth the Queen Mother") on her second visit to the school) and the Design Centre comprising various engineering facilities.[[6]](#cite_note-Wheel-6)

The most recent building work to a school building took place on the school's Performing Arts Centre. The Performing Arts Centre (formerly a gymnasium), was extended to create three spaces: Studio 1 downstairs, Studio 2 upstairs, and the Godfrey Brown Theatre. An entrance was added to the back of the building and a car park was opened. This project cost the school £2.8 million.

The school's library was refurbished in 2001, and was renamed the Philip Sawyer Library (after the former chairman of the governors). It was opened by the then [Poet Laureate](/wiki/Poet_Laureate_of_the_United_Kingdom "Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom") [Andrew Motion](/wiki/Andrew_Motion "Andrew Motion"). The library is situated above Eld Hall, and features a high vaulted roof structure.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Sport")]

### Rowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Rowing")]

The school has a [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") club, the Royal Grammar School Worcester Boat Club, which is based on the [River Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn"). The club is affiliated to [British Rowing](/wiki/British_Rowing "British Rowing") (boat code WRG)[[18]](#cite_note-18) and has produced British champion crews at the [British Rowing Championships](/wiki/British_Rowing_Championships "British Rowing Championships") in 1997, 2000, 2001, 2002 and 2003.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Other Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Grammar_School_Worcester&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other Sports")]

The school offers Rugby union and football to boys and hockey and netball to girls. The school stopped teaching lacrosse in 2010.

Both boys and girls are taught tennis, athletics, swimming and, after a six-year campaign by a former pupil, cricket is now taught to girls as well as boys.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
